5 min read
Just now

--

Series: After Building AI Systems That Assume Agents Will Forget — June Calibration Diaries (Part 1 of 5)
Press enter or click to view image in full size

On June 1, I published Building AI Systems That Assume Agents Will Forget.
That post was about drift.
Documentation that no longer matched reality. Agents confidently referencing stale state. A growing gap between what the system believed and what was actually happening.
The solution was governance: reconciliation loops, durable state, postflight logs, and workflows designed around a simple assumption:
Agents forget. Systems must not.
But June exposed a different problem.
Once governance exists, reality starts fighting back.
The challenge is no longer remembering what happened.
The challenge is determining whether what happened was actually correct.
June was not about memory.
June was about calibration.
What we are actually building
I am building a forensic video analytics system for retail environments.
The long-term goal is straightforward:
Turn surveillance footage into reliable person-level journeys across multiple cameras.
June was not about solving that entire problem.
June focused on establishing trustworthy identities within individual camera views before attempting global identity assembly.
Every stage of the pipeline must answer a deceptively difficult question:
Is this still the same person?
The entire month can be explained by one failure mode.
A shopper exits a location.
A few frames later, another shopper enters nearly the same coordinates.
To a human observer, these are obviously different people.
To a geometry-driven tracker, they can become one continuous identity.
Person A disappears.
Person B inherits the bounding box.
The ID survives.
The person does not.
Everything we calibrated in June exists to catch this failure before it becomes a customer journey.
Frame 721: Person A in their final verified position.
Frame 726: Person B occupies the same location. To a visually blind tracker, this appears to be a continuous track.
Three goals
1. Automatic track purity
Raw ByteTrack IDs are not people.
They are tracking hypotheses.
Before anything becomes a shopper journey, we need confidence that every atomic segment contains exactly one human.
Our definition of purity is intentionally simple:
One real person per folder.
Not “the export succeeded.”
Not “the tracker never crashed.”
One human.
One identity.
One folder.
June focused on building automated mechanisms that detect contamination before it propagates downstream.
2. Faster iteration
The legacy calibration workflow performed healing first and validation later.
That meant waiting for exports to complete before discovering whether a merge should have happened at all.
At calibration scale, that becomes expensive.
Legacy splitter + healer execution:

• ~41.6 seconds per batch

• ~17.0 seconds on Camera 1

• ~28.1 seconds on Camera 5
The goal was to move purity enforcement into generation itself rather than relying on downstream audits.
3. Multi-camera identity
Real shoppers move between cameras.
Eventually Camera 1 and Camera 5 must become one global identity.
The challenge is that cross-camera matching compounds errors already present in atomic tracks.
If camera-local identities are wrong, cross-camera identity resolution simply amplifies those mistakes.
June delivered the architecture:

• segment confidence scoring

• PURE-only linking

• cross-camera validation as a veto layer
The design landed.
The production merge system did not.
That distinction matters.
Calibration only works when the system remains honest about what is unfinished.
The pipeline we built
Raw ByteTrack IDs
→ complementary heal (interleaved fragments)
→ purity-gated atomic heal
→ atomic export (1,177 tracks)
→ complementary pre-puzzle (5 verified stitch pairs)
→ puzzle assembly (703 camera-local person folders)
→ stitch audit at virtual boundaries
→ [next] cross-camera identity linkingA single rule emerged:
Every layer may only merge what the previous layer certified as pure.
Earlier versions of the system merged first and audited later.
June reversed that relationship.
Breakthrough #1: Purity at generation time
The most important change was not a new model.
It was a change in where validation occurs.
Early versions of the pipeline healed first and audited later.
Bad merges could survive until export.
June moved purity checks into the healing process itself.
Every proposed merge now passes through multiple gates:

• co-occupancy checks

• spatial alibi validation

• boundary similarity analysis

• ReID outbid detection
The question changed from:
Was this merge wrong?
to:
Should this merge happen at all?
That shift fundamentally changed the calibration loop.
Measured impact
MetricLegacyPurity-GatedBatch runtime41.6s6.5sCamera 117.0s3.4sCamera 528.1s3.4sOverall improvement — 6.4×
The result was faster iteration and fewer downstream audit failures.
Breakthrough #2: The puzzle graph
The next bottleneck appeared in camera-local identity assembly.
A naive approach compares every atomic track against every other atomic track.
At June scale that produced approximately:
1,177 tracks
≈ 1.17 million pairwise comparisonsMost of those comparisons are impossible.
People cannot teleport.
They cannot appear in arbitrary locations at arbitrary times.
So instead of treating identity assembly as a global search problem, we reframed it as a puzzle.
Tracks become pieces.
Temporal neighbors become candidates.
Only plausible neighbors advance to expensive ReID evaluation.
The result:
~1.17 million comparisons
↓
~19,000 candidate pairs
↓
~98% reductionThe system spends time evaluating likely matches rather than impossible ones.
June by the numbers
Metric - Outcome
Atomic tracks exported1 - 177
Verified stitch pairs - 5
Camera-local person folders assembled - 703
Camera-local folder purity ~ 97.8%
Candidate comparisons 1.17M → ~19K
Comparison reduction ~ 98%
Atomic heal runtime 41.6s → 6.5s
Speedup~6.4×These are calibration outputs, not projections.
Every number comes from completed June runs.
Every unit of work is traceable. Calibration outputs, verification state, and completion criteria are persisted independently of agent context.
The 703 folders reported here are camera-local identities.
Cross-camera identity resolution remains a separate calibration phase and is not included in these metrics.
Current state
Layer - Outcome
Atomic baseline - 1,177 purity-gated tracks
Camera - local identity baseline - 703 camera-local person folders
Camera - local folder purity ~ 97.8%
Cross-camera identity resolution - Designed, not shipped
Phase - CALIBRATINGJune 1 gave us reconciliation.
The rest of June gave us something worth reconciling against.
What June actually taught me
The first phase of this project taught me that agents forget.
The second phase taught me that systems drift toward whatever they measure.
If purity is not measured, it decays.
If provenance is not enforced, it disappears.
If calibration is not continuous, confidence becomes fiction.
The work in June was not really about tracking.
It was about building a workflow where:

• progress is measurable

• regressions are visible

• provenance is preserved

• human review remains ground truth
We still assume the agents will forget.
The difference is that the system now produces enough evidence to determine whether they were right before they did.
